name: Ben — Motel / Divorce
description:
VARIANT — MOTEL / DIVORCE (Sc. 11–21): The lowest, most civilian register. Ben wakes alone in a cheap motel room, 'Unshaven, heavy around the eyes' (Sc. 11), two single beds and an open suitcase. He shaves with an electric razor wearing a shirt and suit jacket (Sc. 12), finishes buttoning a cheap dress shirt and pulls on the jacket to complete an ill-fitting suit, the TV table ringed with beer bottles he bags for the dumpster (Sc. 13–14). His gait up the municipal-court steps is 'heavy and slow' (Sc. 16); across the cafeteria table from Marie he deadpans 'I shaved' (Sc. 18). Palette here: sodium motel light through blinds, cheap gray off-the-rack suit, grief-swollen heavy eyes.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Jonathan Rhys Meyers — cast. Broad-shouldered man in his mid-40s with a mechanic's build and calloused, capable hands; dark, slightly-receding hair, a strong jaw that works with suppressed emotion. A faded black-ink Ace of Hearts tattoo sits on his inner wrist — the mark of a New Mexico crew for whom he was 'a wrench. I fixed problems' (Sc. 227); Waif reads it: 'Black ink is easier to get off... an ace of hearts. Which crew is that? New Mexico?' (Sc. 114). He instinctively pulls on a long-sleeve shirt to hide his tattoos (Sc. 1). Palette: earth tones — faded blue, gray, brown. Contemporary, working-class American.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Motel/Divorce. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: Ben — Cabin / Mechanic
description:
VARIANT — CABIN / MECHANIC (Sc. 26–124): Ben in his element and his exile — flannel, worn jeans, work boots, sleeves that ride up over the tattoos. He guts and rebuilds the cabin: loppers clearing branches from the windows (Sc. 38), floorboards levered over the crawl-space, new wire crimped to old by stove-light (Sc. 51), burnt solenoids soldered (Sc. 124). The faded Ace of Hearts is glimpsed by Terry as Ben peels a fifty from his envelope on the forest road (Sc. 68). He quickdraws six rounds into tomato cans at twelve feet (Sc. 49); builds an improvised bulb-pipe and gets high, catching his own reflection in the black window (Sc. 71–73). Palette here: tungsten cabin gloom, dark flannel, oil and sawdust.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Jonathan Rhys Meyers — cast. Broad-shouldered man in his mid-40s with a mechanic's build and calloused, capable hands; dark, slightly-receding hair, a strong jaw that works with suppressed emotion. A faded black-ink Ace of Hearts tattoo sits on his inner wrist — the mark of a New Mexico crew for whom he was 'a wrench. I fixed problems' (Sc. 227); Waif reads it: 'Black ink is easier to get off... an ace of hearts. Which crew is that? New Mexico?' (Sc. 114). He instinctively pulls on a long-sleeve shirt to hide his tattoos (Sc. 1). Palette: earth tones — faded blue, gray, brown. Contemporary, working-class American.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Cabin. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: Ben — Siege / Post-Reset
description:
VARIANT — SIEGE / POST-RESET (Sc. 185–255): After the first quantum-suicide reset Ben carries a circular scorch mark and welt on his forehead from the revolver misfire — 'A scorch mark and welt. Misfire' (Sc. 185) — which Marie clocks as something darker (Sc. 190). In the house assault he arms himself with an ax, a flashlight duct-taped to the handle, 'pointed ahead of him like a sword' (Sc. 217), a box-cutter jammed in his back pocket. His clothes tear; The Mother tears the flesh of his ankle in her grip and he fights on 'limping, blood spilling onto the floor' (Sc. 224). Bloodied, sweat-soaked and torn, he hacks through The Mother and the Lady in White (Sc. 220–253). Palette here: pale blue firelight, blood-black, adrenal grime.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Jonathan Rhys Meyers — cast. Broad-shouldered man in his mid-40s with a mechanic's build and calloused, capable hands; dark, slightly-receding hair, a strong jaw that works with suppressed emotion. A faded black-ink Ace of Hearts tattoo sits on his inner wrist — the mark of a New Mexico crew for whom he was 'a wrench. I fixed problems' (Sc. 227); Waif reads it: 'Black ink is easier to get off... an ace of hearts. Which crew is that? New Mexico?' (Sc. 114). He instinctively pulls on a long-sleeve shirt to hide his tattoos (Sc. 1). Palette: earth tones — faded blue, gray, brown. Contemporary, working-class American.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Siege. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: The Waif — Arrival / Rain-Soaked
description:
VARIANT — ARRIVAL / RAIN-SOAKED (Sc. 78–95): First seen through the cabin peephole in lashing rain — 'Skin and bones. Dirty and dark-eyed, wearing thin, filthy clothes' (Sc. 78) — her muddy hand smearing the lens. At the door crevice: a cheap cracked dumbphone held out, mud-covered sneakers, the thigh tattoo bare in the rain (Sc. 78–79). In the shower her feet are filthy, toenails painted black, scratches up her legs (Sc. 100). She cuts her sole open on broken glass, blood dripping to the floor (Sc. 93). Palette here: wet grays and grime, mud, shivering exposure.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Antonia Campbell-Hughes (body type). Wiry, thin woman, late 20s to 30s. A tattoo coils up her bare thighs (Sc. 78) and an elaborate caduceus — two snakes coiling around each other — covers her bare back (Sc. 121; 'Two twists of fate, coiled together,' Sc. 239). On her feet, white canvas sneakers with red stripes — identical to the jumper's, the film's central reveal (Sc. 266). She answers to many names: 'Alice. Mary. Junebug. Kit' (Sc. 239). Contemporary drifter.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Arrival. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: The Waif — Showered / Marie's Clothes
description:
VARIANT — SHOWERED / MARIE'S CLOTHES (Sc. 100–239): After washing she wears Marie's borrowed clothes — 'We're the same size. Ain't that funny?' (Sc. 104) — with a bandage coiled on her glass-cut foot (Sc. 97). The caduceus tattoo on her back is revealed when she crawls into Ben's bed (Sc. 121). Her physicality turns mercurial and seductive: a 'kind of sacrilegious movement' swaying to the hymn on the radio (Sc. 99); energized and laughing after the revolver clicks against her temple (Sc. 108, 114); smoking on the child's swing as she lays out her quantum-immortality theory (Sc. 132). Palette here: dry, clean, warm lamplight — grime replaced by uncanny composure.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Antonia Campbell-Hughes (body type). Wiry, thin woman, late 20s to 30s. A tattoo coils up her bare thighs (Sc. 78) and an elaborate caduceus — two snakes coiling around each other — covers her bare back (Sc. 121; 'Two twists of fate, coiled together,' Sc. 239). On her feet, white canvas sneakers with red stripes — identical to the jumper's, the film's central reveal (Sc. 266). She answers to many names: 'Alice. Mary. Junebug. Kit' (Sc. 239). Contemporary drifter.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Showered. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: The Waif — Transformed / One of the Others
description:
VARIANT — TRANSFORMED / ONE OF THE OTHERS (Sc. 254, 270): In the doorway after Marie and Jack die she stands 'Covered in blood,' lighting a cigarette casually (Sc. 254). Then the Others pull her apart in Jack's room — 'joints splitting, sinews tearing... hands and fingers merging into her flesh' (Sc. 269) — and she is remade. She exits the cabin transformed: 'Her gait is strange, like them. Her skin is pale and lifeless. Her eyes are dead' (Sc. 270). She clicks her fingers and the pale fire goes out (Sc. 270). Palette here: ash-pale dead skin, cold blue firelight guttering to black.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Antonia Campbell-Hughes (body type). Wiry, thin woman, late 20s to 30s. A tattoo coils up her bare thighs (Sc. 78) and an elaborate caduceus — two snakes coiling around each other — covers her bare back (Sc. 121; 'Two twists of fate, coiled together,' Sc. 239). On her feet, white canvas sneakers with red stripes — identical to the jumper's, the film's central reveal (Sc. 266). She answers to many names: 'Alice. Mary. Junebug. Kit' (Sc. 239). Contemporary drifter.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Transformed. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: Marie — Domestic / Home Timeline
description:
VARIANT — DOMESTIC / HOME TIMELINE (Sc. 5, 8, 42, 188, 274): The living wife and mother of Ben's own world. Simple academic comfort — cardigans, blouses, practical trousers in warm earth tones; dark hair tied back. She works the transparent equation easel amid a small child's clutter (Sc. 5; identically again after the final reset, Sc. 274), buckles into passenger-seat LaTeX (Sc. 8), phones Ben at the cabin (Sc. 42). After the crash, this timeline's Marie carries limited mobility in one arm — she peels dollar bills one-handed in the court cafeteria (Sc. 18) — the legacy of a denim-sheathed thigh bone driven into her arm. At the sunlit lakeside reunion she is soft and unguarded, sensing only that 'Something is off' (Sc. 188). Palette here: warm domestic tones, morning window light.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Joana Ribeiro — cast. Woman in her late 30s to early 40s, average practical build, dark hair usually tied back, an intelligent, weary face carrying learned grief. Signature image: drawing 'a complete set of equations on a transparent easel in the kitchen' (Sc. 5), later transcribing the formulas into LaTeX from a photo on her laptop in the passenger seat (Sc. 8). Contemporary academic; warm-toned counterpoint to Ben's cold blues.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Domestic. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: Marie — Alternate / Siege Timeline
description:
VARIANT — ALTERNATE / SIEGE TIMELINE (Sc. 198–253): The wife of a world Ben quantum-jumped into — subtly, disturbingly wrong. She carries an old bruise on her shoulder and, when Ben asks how she got it, answers flatly 'You know how I got it' (Sc. 210); her manner is disciplined and flinching — she flinches at his touch (Sc. 200) and waits with Jack for grace before eating (Sc. 203). The mantelpiece holds 'strange and formal' photos, including the Northrup proposal picture (Sc. 204). Under siege she wields the ax against The Mother — 'Stay the fuck away — stay away!' (Sc. 230) — and dies in the crushed pickup cab, 'Face blank. Sides ripped out. Still holding... Jack' (Sc. 253). Palette here: cooler, tenser, warm tones drained toward apprehension.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Joana Ribeiro — cast. Woman in her late 30s to early 40s, average practical build, dark hair usually tied back, an intelligent, weary face carrying learned grief. Signature image: drawing 'a complete set of equations on a transparent easel in the kitchen' (Sc. 5), later transcribing the formulas into LaTeX from a photo on her laptop in the passenger seat (Sc. 8). Contemporary academic; warm-toned counterpoint to Ben's cold blues.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Alternate Timeline. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: Jack — Domestic / Home Timeline
description:
VARIANT — DOMESTIC / HOME TIMELINE (Sc. 3, 8, 188, 277): The warm, curious original. Signature poses: sitting on the edge of his bed in the dark holding a flashlight, waving his palm slowly at the wardrobe mirror — 'It kept moving... in the mirror' (Sc. 3); tracing his finger between the car-window glass and his face — 'I told you I was coming with you' (Sc. 8, reprised as the film's final image, Sc. 277–278); crayon pastels of a dark figure lording over stick people (Sc. 5); tipping a bucket of fish back into the sunlit lake (Sc. 188). Ordinary red-striped sneakers on his feet; in the crash his head is bisected, 'one eye moving in idle reflex' (Sc. 10). Palette here: warm child clutter, flashlight glow, innocence.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Six-year-old boy, small and slight, with an unnerving alertness — eyes that catch reflections adults miss. Ordinary boy's clothes, t-shirt and jeans; his defining item is white canvas sneakers with two red stripes, identical to the jumper's (Sc. 10, 265). Shot at child's eye level, 35mm. Contemporary American kid — innocence with premonition underneath.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Domestic. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: Jack — Alternate / Siege Timeline
description:
VARIANT — ALTERNATE / SIEGE TIMELINE (Sc. 176, 190, 202–253): Edgier, obedient, wary of the father wearing Ben's face. In the first alternate return he shields his mother from the stranger — 'Stay away from her. Stay away!' (Sc. 176). In the reversed-house timeline he kneels before a computer game and asks permission — 'I've done my homework. So I can play, right?' (Sc. 202) — tensing under Ben's affection (Sc. 202, 209) and waiting for grace before eating (Sc. 203). He dies in the crushed cab, 'Half his head missing, like the accident' (Sc. 253). It is this Jack who quietly hides the rosewood box under the cabin bed (Sc. 192). Palette here: computer-screen glow on a small, tense face; disciplined stillness.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): Six-year-old boy, small and slight, with an unnerving alertness — eyes that catch reflections adults miss. Ordinary boy's clothes, t-shirt and jeans; his defining item is white canvas sneakers with two red stripes, identical to the jumper's (Sc. 10, 265). Shot at child's eye level, 35mm. Contemporary American kid — innocence with premonition underneath.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Alternate Timeline. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: The Mother — Initial / Intact Attack Form
description:
VARIANT — INITIAL / INTACT ATTACK FORM (Sc. 115–118, 145, 210–211): Her upright, menacing incarnation. She first steps in front of the pale fire and stands motionless (Sc. 115), then trudges forward and steps through the fire, its strange flames not burning her (Sc. 117). Her mouth opens 'unnaturally wide' as she presses her face to the window bars (Sc. 118). In the suburb she stands silhouetted by burning garden bushes, hands and lips moving in silent incantation, her head snapping forward (Sc. 210–211). An earlier incarnation wears a nurse's uniform in Waif's intensive-care doorway — Waif's first sighting of the others (Sc. 145). Palette here: long dark coat, greasy hair, blue flame behind her.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): '50s, wild-haired, head low in a long man's coat' (Sc. 115), standing eerily still. Greasy hair covers her face; she moves in brown boots. A timeless folk-horror silhouette dropped into a contemporary suburb and forest. Palette: coal-dark coat against pale blue firelight.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Initial/Attack. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: The Mother — Severed / Reassembling Form
description:
VARIANT — SEVERED / REASSEMBLING FORM (Sc. 220–228, 230, 247): Her wounded, regenerating state. She does not bleed — cut, her skin 'breaks like pieces of tough paper' (Sc. 220); struck, her limbs wilt and deflate 'with a hissing sound like a burst balloon' and a crackle of static (Sc. 220, 224). Hacked apart, she is later found 'reassembling herself. Severed parts inflating and locking together' (Sc. 228), her staccato movements smoothing as she re-forms into 'a more rigid and upright form' (Sc. 230); an ax sticks fast in her arm when Marie swings (Sc. 230). Render her as a body of paper and trapped air, seams splitting and inflating, never wet. Palette here: torn pale matter, coal coat, static-blue light.

COMMON BASE (invariant, shared by all variants): '50s, wild-haired, head low in a long man's coat' (Sc. 115), standing eerily still. Greasy hair covers her face; she moves in brown boots. A timeless folk-horror silhouette dropped into a contemporary suburb and forest. Palette: coal-dark coat against pale blue firelight.
notes: Bifurcated variant — Severed/Reassembling. From fountain + Claude Opus

name: Woman in White
description: Introduced from the ground up: grimy bare feet walking toward the fire, "bony ankles" catching "the trails of a white dress", then "a pale ethereal figure" joining The Mother in the flame-light — the script names her THE LADY IN WHITE. Her hands are wiry and skeletal; she squeezes "unnaturally" through a smashed wall gap, resuming shape on the other side, and when Ben hacks off her dangling legs they "drop off, devoid of matter" — her legless trunk drags itself forward "insect-like" on its elbows. On the pickup she crawls up the hood "like a spider", tears through the roof with "sharp elongated fingers" and "long razor-like fingernails slicing through the aluminum," gaining mass until the cab collapses inward. After the kill she is "draped across it like some strange expressionist sculpture," slipping off "like a fattened predator, heavy of belly". Gothic ghost iconography subverted into pure predator; white dress against night and pale blue flame.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus (subsection match)

name: The Others
description: Pale, human-shaped figures with blank, featureless faces, "faces flickering in the pale light" as they mass outside the cabin. Their bodies are hollow husks: a shot Other's skull cracks open "exposing hollowness"; a struck head "crumples like an eggshell, vaporous and hissing"; boiling oil melts one's face. They snarl and snap their heads forward trying to bite, pour through open doors like liquid, and pull Waif apart — "joints splitting, sinews tearing… hands and fingers *merging* into her flesh". When Ben surrenders they circle him "like hyenas," then refuse him — "Like he's poisoned meat. Inedible" — and form a silent two-line gauntlet of blank tracking faces leading to the cabin door. Afterward they disperse outward "like the cabin is the hub and they are the spokes". No distinct clothing — gaunt pale silhouettes against blue firelight. Timeless, unplaceable; palette of ash-pale flesh and cold blue.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: The Man
description: Waif's lost anchor — introduced as "a dark, backlit figure" offering his hand down an institution corridor; "He became her oak tree", lifting her whole weight in bed. Older NYC businessman defined by silhouette: emerging from a revolving door as a door guard drags the screaming Waif away, standing before a floor-to-ceiling glass wall over New York City when she comes for him with a revolver. In the corrected memory she doesn't shoot herself in front of him — she fires once, shattering the glass, backs to the edge, and lets herself fall while he stands composed. Immaculate business suit, unhurried confidence of ownership. Corporate grays and glass blues; 16mm flashback register.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: Querry Preston
description: Q.R. Preston, author of *The Rules of Quantum Immortality*, dead 1946–2014 per his leaf-strewn garden gravestone. Los Alamos-adjacent physicist and his own armorer: his lab holds vintage radiology equipment, capacitor banks, lathes, and metal gun barrels under plastic. In the robbery flashback he emerges from the basement holding the revolver, aims it at the intruders, then turns it on his own head and pulls the trigger — nothing, again and again — until Ben snaps his wrist, takes the gun ("Remember to load it next time, pops-") and it fires, blowing his brains out. Imagined as 70s, silver-haired, gaunt and patrician, in nightclothes for the break-in. Gothic register, warm candle-and-brass tones; 16mm flashback texture.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: Joyce Preston
description: Querry's widow — an elderly, well-kept woman first met "Dressed for a Sunday dinner" beside the gravestone she is having chiselled for both of them: "I'm not the undead… Save the state some dollars". She carries a scar on her crown from the robbery cosh — "They coshed me here on the crown" — and recounts, dry-eyed, watching her husband point a gun at himself that "wouldn't work" until a robber took it from him. In the flashback she is smacked in the mouth with a cosh by masked Waif while Ben binds her hands. She leafs through the felt-covered book in her mansion kitchenette and shows Ben the striplit lab. Mansion interior tones: dark wood, lamplight; flashback moments in 16mm texture.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: Jamie
description: Small-town hardware store owner, plausibly 50s, solid and practical — flannel or workwear shirt behind the counter of Last Chance Supply, among coolers, pegboard tool racks, and a glass gun cabinet. Friendly but shrewd: refuses to buy a used gun ("federal records are a pain in the ass") yet points Ben to a cash-sale gun show, fetches a blue box of ammo from the back, and produces a clear bottle of bootleg moonshine — "it's an art, not a science. I'd guess one-twenty, one-thirty". In the second timeline he works a backroom gunsmith bench, testing the revolver's hammer mechanism — "revolvers… they're a hammer and a hunk of metal" — and offers to buy it: "Half my custom". His softest register is with his son: "RICKY! Sorry, friend". Contemporary rural Upstate New York; warm tungsten interior tones.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: Ricky
description: Sixteen-year-old boy in a wheelchair, still and silent, face lit by television glow amid the hardware shelves of his father's store; his hands grip the remote, turning the TV louder and louder as Jamie calls his name. After the first reset he moves down the aisle in a motorized chair with a mechanical whir — new wheels that Jamie says don't exist yet: a walking Mandela effect. In the second timeline he is gone entirely — T.R. bought the store "after his son died". Ordinary teenage clothes — hoodie, t-shirt, sneakers; composed as a tableau, the motionless observer in the frame's middle distance. Contemporary; muted store interior with the TV as key light.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: Terry
description: Small-town dealer in a baseball cap, lurking "deeper in the shadows" of the alley between shops, hands stuffed in jacket, while a teenage boy fronts for him — "Answer the boy". Drives a Chevrolet with tinted windows that tails Ben onto the forest road. In the confrontation he swings first and folds instantly — face slammed against his own car, arm jacked high, screaming "Let me fucking go!" — then rattles off his stock: "perks, oxys, blues… eight-balls, hot-rails". He recognizes the faded Ace of Hearts on Ben's wrist and reacts, later branding him "narc." Cheap contemporary rural streetwear: scuffed boots, layered jacket, cap shading his eyes. Palette: gray-brown alley grime, forest-road gloom.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: Schrödinger
description: A black stray cat, lean and watchful, introduced prowling the windowsill exactly as Ben studies the book's diptych of a black cat dead-and-alive in a box. She stakes out the porch, gets a bowl of Catfish Catfood and a "Now fuck off. You're not getting in", kills a rat and displays it proudly, and lies placid — uncannily so — when Waif seals her into a cardboard box for the live Schrödinger's-cat demonstration with Jack's blue ball as the "poison". She screeches and circles at the door the moment the pale fire lights, sensing the Others before Ben does. After the reset she recognizes a Ben this world has never met — "Seems like it knows you". Often framed half in warm lamplight, half in cold night: the living hinge between inside and outside. Naturalistic, never cute.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: The Revolver
description: "A STRANGE REVOLVER on velvet backing" — matte steel, a sigil-like glyph on the side, a heptagonal cylinder with six voids and a sealed seventh chamber. Not a modified antique but an engineered object from Querry Preston's own lathe: cold, precise, purpose-built to gamble with quantum decay. A Geiger counter's clicking *saturates* over the sealed chamber; Waif finally chisels that chamber off on the cabin desktop so it will "fire every time". Photograph it like scientific dread: cold steel, dark backgrounds, the counter's wand nearby.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus (subsection match)

name: Red-Striped Sneakers
description: White canvas sneakers with red stripes — childlike, bright, cheap. Canon appearances: on the jumper's legs wedged beside Ben in the wreck, "sneakers with red stripes… Red-striped sneaker on a distended foot"; mud-covered on Waif at the cabin door; revealed when the falling Waif's coat rips away in the air before her body smashes into the Forester; and finally on Waif's feet in the cabin — "Look at mine, Ben… It was me. I was the jumper". The single most important color accent in the film — red-on-white against a desaturated world.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus

name: Pale Blue Fire
description: "A pale blue shade, a strange pattern like St. Elmo's Fire" — burning steady through wind and downpour, no heat to a reaching hand, extinguishing without smoke or char. It consumes the suburban garden bushes as The Mother incants, glows through shutter-gaps as the Others mass, lights the climactic yard massacre, and dies when the transformed Waif clicks her fingers. Render as cold plasma, never campfire orange: blue-white cores, faint violet edges, the only saturated cold accent in the film's palette.
notes: Updated from fountain + Claude Opus (subsection match)

name: T.R.
description: Owner of T.R. General Supplies in the second timeline — "50s, wiry" — appearing from the backroom of a store that used to be Jamie's: "he sold me this place years ago. After his son died". Flat, incurious, transactional: "Cash only." A vape stand where the gun cabinet was. He is the human face of timeline drift — same counter, different world. Contemporary rural store owner; workwear, wiry frame, fluorescent retail light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Teenage Boy (alley)
description: A rural teenager in the alley between Broken Bow shops — "Hey mister… You looking to buy something?" — the front-of-house for Terry, who lurks deeper in shadow. Face still soft with childhood but performing hardness; hands in pockets, positioned at the boundary between the postcard main street and the alley's dark. Contemporary small-town poverty; gray daylight.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Door Guard
description: Broad NYC office-building security — the blocking body that grabs Waif as The Man walks past her from the revolving door, her face "a mask of desperation and pain" as she screams after him. Dark suit or security blazer; physical and impersonal. 16mm flashback texture, corporate lobby glass and steel behind him.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Institution Guard
description: A state-institution guard in drab uniform, keyring heavy on the belt — he stares at Waif as she sweeps the corridor in patient overalls; in a patient room she unclips his keyring mid-act, one hand sliding up his side. Complacent and exploitable. Fluorescent corridor light, institutional green-gray walls. Flashback register.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Nurse (Mother incarnation)
description: "A figure in the doorway. Dressed in a nurse's uniform, but resembling The Mother. Staring at Waif" in her intensive-care bed — Waif's first sighting of the others. The wrongness is postural: too still, at the threshold, face slack under the bandage-white light. Whites and pale greens gone slightly gray; hospital-clean turned uncanny.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Psychiatrist
description: A figure whose face we never see, led into Waif's padded room; a chair is placed in the corner, and the Psychiatrist sets *The Rules of Quantum Immortality* in front of the straitjacketed Waif — "I was given the book". Compose faceless: back of head, shadowed profile, hands and book in focus. Institutional flashback register; the book's felt cover the only warm object in the frame.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Ben's Lawyer & Marie's Lawyer
description: Two lawyers flanking their clients across a conference-room table in the Municipal Court Building — off-the-rack suits, folders, procedural monotone: "He was a joint contributor to the mortgage…" vs. "his record will be seen by the judge." Marie's lawyer is first heard O.S. over the courthouse steps; Ben's silently shakes his head when Marie asks to talk alone. Later seen THRU GLASS overseeing the signing. Cold institutional neutrals, fluorescent light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Motel Neighbor
description: A motel long-termer parked on a deck chair outside his room, beside an outdoor pool covered in autumn leaves, watching Ben carry a trash bag of beer bottles to the dumpster. Weathered, unhurried, incurious. Transient-America palette: beige stucco, leaf-choked pool blue, morning light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Elderly Neighbor
description: An elderly man at the picket fence of the first alternate timeline, holding a hedge trimmer, watching Ben fail to turn his own key — "The people who live there, I kinda look out for them". He tweaks a curtain to catch Ben returning, calls the police, and pile-drives Ben against the wall in a bear hug when the screen door is breached. His stunned "That's… that's *not* possible" at "I'm her husband" is the timeline's tell. Cardigan, slacks; tended-suburbia protector. Overcast domestic light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Marie's New Partner
description: Never clearly seen — canon keeps him as presence: someone moving behind the glass paneling as Marie takes Ben's call, another voice on the line, a dark-windowed Prius turning into the garage, Marie's "It's not your concern". Best used as implied — a silhouette, a second car, a coat on the hook. Warm domestic tones Ben is locked out of.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Barber
description: Older Broken Bow barber glimpsed sweeping his porch in the Main Street drive-by, between the diner, bank, and tackle shop, "brick facades softened by lake winters". White or striped shirt, sleeves rolled. Small-town Americana, faded postcard palette.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Diner Locals
description: Background townsfolk of a lake town past its peak — retirees, workers in flannel, flags on shop fronts. *Canon note:* the fountain never goes inside the diner; use these figures on sidewalks, porches, and store aisles rather than in booths. In the second timeline, swap in neon cannabis signs and a woman pushing a can trolley. Warm tungsten against gray lake light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Cops
description: Suburban patrol officers barreling through the smashed front door — too late, siren lights already flooding the room and the In Memoriam photo of Ben; two cruisers parked out front; later a lone siren sweeping blue-red light across Ben's pickup hidden in a scenic pullout. Standard contemporary NY-state uniforms; light-bar strobe as the visual signature.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Big Bat & The Rats
description: Cabin fauna as dread punctuation: a BIG BAT that rushes Ben from behind the closed bathroom door and clatters around the lodge room before screeching out the front door; a maggot-swarmed dead rat in the swing-top trash can, found by flashlight beam; a live rat's face inches from Ben's in the attic, squeezing its body out through the roofing; Schrödinger's mangled porch kill. Shot close, sudden, naturalistic. ## Key Props
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Quantum Revolver
description: "A STRANGE REVOLVER on velvet backing" — matte steel, a sigil-like glyph on the side, a heptagonal cylinder with six voids and a sealed seventh chamber. Not a modified antique but an engineered object from Querry Preston's own lathe: cold, precise, purpose-built to gamble with quantum decay. A Geiger counter's clicking *saturates* over the sealed chamber; Waif finally chisels that chamber off on the cabin desktop so it will "fire every time". Photograph it like scientific dread: cold steel, dark backgrounds, the counter's wand nearby.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Rosewood Box
description: "An ORNATE ROSEWOOD BOX" with velvet-lined interior shaped to the revolver — a reliquary, not storage. Discovered by flashlight in the crawl-space, crushing the dead electrical wire against a wooden support; pried open with a screwdriver jabbed into the corner. It rides in the pickup foot well tucked to Ben's side and is hidden under the cabin bed by Jack. Dark red-brown polished grain gone dusty; warm browns against flashlight beams and dust motes.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Rules of Quantum Immortality (book)
description: A slight felt-covered volume, no jacket, different from the physics textbooks it hides among on the cabin shelf. Title page: "*THE RULES OF QUANTUM IMMORTALITY* by Q.R. PRESTON"; pages yellowed "but seemingly unread, as if just cut," with strange diagrams of branching realities and a diptych of a black cat dead and alive in a box device. Verso: a strange serpent-like insignia over "PUBLISHED BY PAIMON PRESS, 1475 SHORE ROAD, NORTH HAVEN, N.Y.", and a branching diagram "like a bare winter oak" from a point marked EVENT HORIZON. Waif calls it "a classic… an info-hazard"; a faceless psychiatrist first gave it to her in a padded room. Warm lamplight, cracked spine, diagram pages fanned open.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Ax
description: Taken down from a wall of nail-hung tools in the suburban basement; Ben duct-tapes a flashlight to the handle, making a light-weapon, and climbs the stairs "ax pointed ahead of him like a sword". In the siege it bisects The Mother's wrist, hacks the Lady in White's dangling legs until they "drop off, devoid of matter", splits her head through a wall gap, and sticks fast in The Mother's arm when Marie swings it. Always shown working, never heroic. Dull steel, oiled wood, silver tape.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Geiger Counter
description: Part of the attic's nuclear preparedness kit; found clicking on the kitchenette worktop beside the revolver — as Ben moves it away the clicks slow to background, over the sealed chamber they *saturate*. Handheld, consumer-grade, wand on a coiled cable — hardware-store science confirming the impossible. Contemporary, utilitarian.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Transparent Glass Equation Easel
description: A freestanding sheet of glass dense with handwritten equations, standing incongruously in the suburban kitchen — and again, identically, after the final reset. Marie photographs it and transcribes the formulas into LaTeX on her laptop in the moving car. Marie behind it, mid-thought, math floating over her face; clean glass, morning window light, domestic clutter beyond.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Jack's Crayon Drawings
description: "Crayon pastels of a dark figure, lording over stick people" pasted to the fridge; the same distinctive drawings paper the walls of his preserved cabin room, the camera drawing close to the DARK FIGURE; glimpsed as loose drawings on the floor through window slats of the alternate-timeline house; one pinned drawing "in Jack's style" survives every reset. Bright waxy crayon primaries depicting something that should not be in a child's picture. Taped curling paper, domestic surfaces, dread by innocence.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Jack's Flashlight
description: A small flashlight whose flicker under the bedroom door is what draws Ben in — the loop's recurring opening image (Sc. 2–3, repeated at the reset, Sc. 272). Jack holds it sitting on the edge of the bed, facing the wardrobe mirror. Warm narrow beam against blue-dark domestic night.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Cassette Recorder + Story Tape
description: A cassette recorder in a box under Jack's cabin bed, among Spanish language tapes — one tape in color, labeled "Story tape" in a child's scrawl with a picture. Ben presses play, gets tape hiss then Jack's voice — "Once upon a time, there was a man who lived in a tree…" — and drops it "like it's something hot". Drunk and high, he finally listens to the whole parable of the man who "thought the tree was the whole world" and the black bird saying "you have to go down now," then hurls his glass at the wall. 1990s plastic technology; smudged labels, worn buttons.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Family Photo
description: The family portrait — Ben, Jack, Marie — on the cabin wall, later wrapped in paper on the desk until Waif's dirty fingers unwrap and stain it. Ben looks to it as he refuses the gun the first time. In the alternate house the mantelpiece pictures are "strange and formal," including a proposal photo "Outside Northrup"; on the wall hangs an IN MEMORIAM PHOTO of Ben, lit by siren lights. A framed picture of the family falls from the wall at the exact moment Ben decides to die instead of erase them. Slightly warm, slightly faded print tones.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Toy Car
description: A toy car with a sharp tail-fin lying in the kitchen's path; in the first pass Ben's foot "just misses it" and he displays it to Marie — "Would not have been a good start to the day". After the final reset his right foot lands on the tail fin, buckling him, drawing a drop of blood — the loop's micro-variation made flesh. Shot close and low: painted metal, floorboards, morning light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Small Blue Rubber Ball
description: Jack's small blue ball fished from behind the cabin armchair and set in an otherwise empty packing box; repurposed by Waif as the "can of poison" dropped in beside Schrödinger in the box demonstration, then tossed under the bed for the cat to chase. Single saturated blue accent in a brown room.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Waif's Notebook
description: Declared at the door — "I got my phone, I got my notebook". She scribbles in it while her foot is being dressed, "pages soaked, paper thin and ink running"; Ben later opens it to find "a dense ink scrawl. Wild and overlapping. Indecipherable notes". The physical record of too many lives. Held in stained fingers; rain-damp paper texture.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Cheap Cracked Dumbphone
description: The dead candy-bar phone Waif holds out through the door crevice — "I tried calling for a cab… I think the battery is dead now as well". She folds one shut after her threat-call to the house — "See you real soon". Wet black plastic, spiderwebbed screen catching porch light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cosh
description: The blunt weapon masked Waif swings into Joyce's mouth in the robbery flash; Joyce remembers being "coshed here on the crown". Short, leather-wrapped, old-school heist hardware. 16mm flashback grain.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Baseball Bat
description: Picked up in the corridor for Ben's nightly sweep — windows, triple locks, "click, click, click" — then eased down outside his door (Sc. 2; repeated at the reset, Sc. 272). Domestic paranoia in object form. Scuffed wood against hallway shadows.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Box-Cutter
description: Grabbed off the basement bench and stuck in Ben's back pocket; when The Mother pins him by the ax handle, his fingers strain, feed it into his grip, and he "JABS and SLICES" her paper-tearing face. A mechanic's tool turned last resort. Yellow or gray handle, work-worn.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Fire Poker
description: The porch's first-reach weapon: Ben takes it before entering the thumping cabin, carries it across creaking floorboards, grabs it instead of the gun when investigating the pale fire — Waif *clocks* the aborted reach for the drawer — and smashes it again and again on The Mother's reaching hand. Black cast iron with a hooked tip against cedar siding.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Battery Lamp
description: The battery-powered lamp set beside the wire run in the crawl-space, casting the "pool of light" Ben carries toward Jack's room in his dream; the strobing kitchen lamp that lights Waif's bleeding foot and the glass-tweezing; held up to track the telephone cable into the treeline through lashing rain. Cold flat white against warm woodburner glow.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Moonshine Bottle & Smudged Glasses
description: Jamie's clear bottle of bootleg — "one-twenty, one-thirty. That sorta range". Ben resists once, then buys two bottles with two raised fingers and necks a drink in the parking lot. It doubles as antiseptic splashed on Waif's cut foot and social lubricant — "Go get some glasses": two smudged glasses filled at the desk before the confession. Clear liquor, greasy glass, cabin gloom.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Dime
description: Slid from the table by Waif for the superposition lesson: tossed, covered — "it's heads and tails at the same time" — revealed tails as the detuned radio murmurs crosstalk between stations. Macro close-up territory: worn silver, wood grain, lamplight.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Ace of Hearts Tattoo
description: A faded Ace of Hearts on Ben's wrist, glimpsed by Terry as Ben peels a fifty from his envelope — Terry *reacts*. Waif reads it in the cabin: "Black ink is easier to get off… I think I saw an ace of hearts. Which crew is that? New Mexico? Long way from home Benny". Prison-adjacent linework softened by years; black ink on weathered skin.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Caduceus Tattoo
description: Revealed when Waif crawls into Ben's bed: "An elaborate caduceus tattoo on her back, two snakes coiling around each other" — the twin-souls emblem made skin ("Two twists of fate, coiled together," Sc. 239). A second tattoo coils up her bare thighs. Ink on grimy pale skin, lamplight.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Wardrobe Mirror
description: The tall wardrobe mirror Jack and Ben face together as Jack waves his palm — "It kept moving… in the mirror" — and later father and reflection wave at each other over the sleeping boy. It recurs in Ben's cabin dream, where JACK appears beside him in the reflection, waving, and in the reset. Ben also confronts mirrors at his worst: the roulette chair set before the cabin mirror, the misfire scorch checked "IN A MIRROR". Slightly foxed glass, night-blue room, toys doubled in reflection.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Mechanic's Tools
description: Ben's trade in objects, always process-accurate: flatbar levering floorboards, utility knife cutting the wire jacket shallow to corroded copper, voltage meter, lineman's pliers severing the run; crimps joining new wire to old by stove-light; solder replacing burnt solenoids; and mid-siege, a screwdriver tip bridging battery post to starter terminal — SPARK, engine. Oily steel, work light, honest labor.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Subaru Forester
description: The gray family car with Jack's child seat, Marie's laptop open in the passenger seat. Its roof caves in "in a split second" under the jumper — shockwave of blood and gore, airbag, horn — then it's rear-ended. After the reset it stands in the cabin driveway "_Undamaged_"; it carries the family's escape from the suburban siege and to the cabin. Its condition is the loop's odometer. Ordinary suburban silver-gray.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Ford F-250 Super Duty
description: Ben's working truck, parked beside the Forester, with an old GPS deck he feeds "1475 SHORE ROAD" and a portable radio riding shotgun. Its solenoids are shorted by the Others — dash lights, no engine, two cables black and burnt under the hood in the rain. In the climax the Lady in White tears through its roof, the cab crumpling inward, "entombing Marie and Jack". Utilitarian dark paint, mud-flecked.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Chisel
description: Waif's tool at the cabin desk, hammered "again and again" until the radioactive seventh chamber breaks off the revolver — she holds the fragment up: "Without it, it will fire every time". Close-up: scarred tool steel, desperate hands, lamplight.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Nuclear Attack Preparedness Kit
description: An attic cardboard box, "relics of a long-dead owner", opened on the lodge table: an EMERGENCY MANUAL with a cartoon family practicing duck-and-cover, IODINE PILLS, WATER PURIFICATION BARS, a DOSIMETER PEN, FIRST AID PACKET, a small GEIGER COUNTER — and three dog tags enameled with names: "Enough for a family". Cold-War survivalism rhyming with Ben's fortress instincts. Yellowed cardboard, civil-defense graphics, dull metal tags.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Rotary Phone / Landline + Address Book
description: The cabin's rotary phone with an old paper address book beside it — "That's new. Rotary phone?" / "Still works". Ben cradles it on his neck while inspecting the revolver, dials nine… one… as a bluff Waif calls, and its dial tone dies and returns with the Others' interference. Black bakelite-style body, coiled cord, lamplight.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Portable Radio
description: Rides shotgun in the pickup; plays blues while Ben works the loppers; pours out an a cappella trio's "Washed in the Blood of Jesus" as Waif sways her sacrilegious dance; is detuned between stations by Waif for the superposition murmur. The truck's dash radio cuts in and out unfixably before the loop and comes through "loud and clear… which is strange" after the first reset. Scuffed portable with dial and antenna; tungsten cabin light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Catfish Catfood
description: Cans of CATFISH CATFOOD from Last Chance Supply — the mascot "resembles his unwelcome guest"; Ben knocks a can into his basket and sets a bowl on the porch: "Now fuck off. You're not getting in". Cheap label art, cartoon cat-fish mascot; porch-light still life.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Loppers
description: A large pair of loppers, blades closing and snapping on branches as Ben clears growth from the cabin windows, radio playing blues, Schrödinger spying from the foliage. Long-handled, red-gripped, work-worn.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Improvised Bulb Pipe (drug paraphernalia)
description: Built with mechanic's method: a bulb unscrewed until the filament dies (Schrödinger's eyes lit by the flash, Sc. 71), base tapped off with a screwdriver leaving a hollow glass shell, a lantern's steel air tube washed of kerosene at the sink while Ben catches his own reflection, then the blackened bulb and tube as he smokes, branches tapping the windows. Later swept into a wastebasket before Waif enters. Handle with documentary restraint: process close-ups, no glamor.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Terry's Drug Baggies
description: Small bags of powder from Terry's jacket — "perks, oxys, blues… eight-balls, hot-rails" — two bags of "powdered-down shit" for a bargained-down fifty. Crumpled plastic in a shaking hand; forest-road gray light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Blue Ammunition Box
description: The small blue box Jamie fetches from the back room, riding beside the gun case on the passenger seat; source of the single loaded round for the roulette and the fistfuls Ben stuffs into his jacket before the siege. Cardboard blue, brass gleam.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Tomato Cans
description: Arranged in a row on a log at twelve feet; six quickdrawn shots and the "cans rupture in a flash of red" — grocery-store viscera foreshadowing the crash. Ben scoops the spent cartridges after. Daylight clearing, red spatter on bark.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Ben's Cash Envelope
description: Divorce-poor finances in an envelope: three folded dollars from Marie in the cafeteria, a fifty peeled out for Terry — the motion that exposes the Ace of Hearts, bills counted out at Jamie's register. Creased paper, working man's wallet economy.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Saucepan of Boiling Oil
description: Siege improvisation: a burner lit, a saucepan filled from a bottle of olive oil, then slung out the kitchenette window — an Other howls and retreats, face glimpsed *melting*. Steam, spatter, blue-black night beyond the window.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Bedtime Book ("The Red Shoes")
description: The fairy tale Ben reads in the blanket fort: "She was forced to dance… over fields and meadows… She danced over the churchyard, though the dead did not dance… they had better things to do" — Andersen's cursed dancer rhyming with the red-striped sneakers. A worn illustrated storybook in a father's hands, toy-lamp glow.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Blanket Fort + Toy Lamp
description: A blanket fort in alternate-timeline Jack's bedroom, Ben hunched inside next to Jack, lit by a toy lamp — the loop's one unguarded moment of father-son warmth, ending with Ben carrying the sleeping boy to bed as Marie watches from the door. Draped blankets, warm pocket of light in a dark room.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Pliers
description: Found under Waif's hospital pillow — planted, perhaps; used to shear the rubber housing off a cable in a storage room before she sparks the bare copper against her arm, blowing her across the room. Institutional steel; flashback grain.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Ben's House Keys
description: The loop's litmus object: Ben's key that will not turn in his own front door, tried "again, and again" under the Neighbor's stare, punched through screen-door netting instead — then, one timeline later, "the door unlatches. The keys work". Also: Marie unscrewing the cabin keys from her ring in the parking lot. Close on hands, brass, latch hardware.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Jack's Computer Game
description: Alternate-timeline Jack kneeling before a screen, pausing the game and looking at the floor when his father enters: "I've done my homework. So I can play, right?". A child's obedience where affection should be. Screen-glow on a small tense face.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Exterior / Porch
description: Revealed as cedars part on the access road: "Cedar-siding browned like old pennies. Windows shuttered internally and glass lined with metal bars". A heavy door with three locks; a fire poker resting on the porch; a child's double swing in the yard that catches Ben's eye, that he tries to wrench out only to claw down to concrete supports, sagging onto one seat beside its empty twin, and where Waif smokes and delivers her radioactive-chamber theory. Autumn: gray light through branches, storm rain at night, the fire pit's glow. Secret Window / Session 9 register; 35mm, muted earth tones.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Fire Pit
description: The stone pit that lights itself: flames "a pale blue shade, a strange pattern like St. Elmo's Fire" burning despite wind and rain, casting no heat against Ben's outstretched hand. It extinguishes without smoke — "Nothing is even charred" — and is "cool to his touch" moments after burning. Night exterior; blue flame the only light against black pines.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Main Lodge Room
description: "Dusty desk, portable radio, bookshelf. Sagging sofa near a cast-iron woodburner and TV. A master bed. Family portrait on the wall". Floorboards creak; at night, deadbolts and internal wooden shutters make it "a lamp-lit tomb of wood". Key stagings: the felt book found among physics textbooks; floorboards levered over the crawl-space; the roulette chair before the mirror interrupted by BANG BANG at the door; Waif's split-screen suicide demonstration; the chisel, the sneaker reveal, and the gun in Ben's mouth. Warm tungsten and deep shadow; claustrophobic wide-lens interior feel.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Kitchenette
description: "Trees pressed against the glass. A portable gas stove in the corner, the hot plates layered with mildew"; a swing-top trash can breeding flies over a dead rat found by flashlight; a gurgling Keurig and a washing machine for Waif's rapid-washed clothes. The revolver and clicking Geiger counter side by side on the worktop; boiling oil on the burner in the siege; the back door Waif opens to let an Other pour through. Neglect made visceral; sickly daylight or lamp gloom, green-brown palette.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Bathroom
description: Trash bags duct-taped over the broken window, glass dustpanned into a bag; the cabinet with tweezers and a blue child's toothbrush that slams the door on Ben's composure — the toothbrush by which Waif deduces the dead child. The shower that spits and chokes before flowing over Waif's filthy feet. Cold tile, taped plastic breathing in the wind.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Jack's Room
description: "A mausoleum. Toys and books all untouched. Jack's distinctive crayon drawings pasted on the walls" — Ben can't cross the threshold, breath tightening; when he finally packs it, he pulls pictures, folds clothes, and finds the tape recorder box under the bed. In his dream it becomes the old house's room, "spacious and impossibly wide for the cabin," with Jack waving in the mirror. In the climax a shadow moves under this bed and arms grab Waif; the Others pull her apart here. Dust motes in weak window light; tender and unsettling.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Crawlspace
description: Under levered floorboards: the electric wire run lit by the battery lamp, copper dark and corroded under a shallowly cut jacket. Ben's flashlight finds the ornate rosewood box "crushing the wire against a wooden support" — planted there, we learn, by a previous Ben, "next to bad wiring you'd have to fix". Flashlight beam through dust; near-black warm browns; the cabin's hidden organ.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Attic
description: "Light from the hatch cuts the dark… Cobwebs hang from wooden struts. Relics of a long-dead owner strewn around" — Ben in a dust-mask, dragging newspaper bundles that slam down in plumes of dust, crawling prone under a crossbeam to a box that hides a rat's face. The box holds the nuclear preparedness kit. Shafted light, streaming dust, gable gaps.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Cabin — Surrounding Forest / Access Road
description: White pine and spruce thick enough "to choke the sunset" on the worn road in; a narrow forest road "encroached by cedars"; branches tapping "morse against a window" at night. After the pale fire, "shadows moving in the trees" and, in the siege, Others massing at the treeline and dispersing outward "like the cabin is the hub and they are the spokes". Nocturnal woodland, lamp beams, supernatural-thriller atmosphere.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Forest Clearing (test-fire)
description: Ben's proving grounds: tomato cans on a log, twelve feet, six quickdrawn shots, red rupture, brass scooped up; after the reset, six shots ripping bark off a tree at ten feet, reloaded and fired again — testing a gun that "should" fail. Daylight through pines, gunsmoke hanging; competence that unsettles its owner.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Lake
description: The cold lake glistening in low sun below the highway vista; private docks among cattail marshes at the town approach. At the lakeside after the reset, "ethereal, with light dappling on the water," Marie and Jack at the shore, Jack catching fish in a bucket and tipping them back; the uphill forest trail with Jack on Ben's shoulders and Schrödinger weaving between his legs. Silver water, gray-gold light, one warm memory in the palette.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Exterior / Street
description: The family home the camera drifts up to as the family emerges, F-250 and gray Forester parked. In the first alternate timeline it reads wrong by being *better*: garden tended, no for-sale sign, shutters pulled, a fresh flowerbed with small handprints in the clay that Ben traces with his fingers. In the second timeline the neighborhood around it has hollowed: "Boarded up houses. Overgrown gardens and for sale signs. Like an exodus". Contemporary; overcast domestic normalcy over dread.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Master Bedroom
description: Where the film begins and re-begins: "Eyes snap open… He assesses the room: four walls and windows with blinds. His wife MARIE sleeps beside him". In the siege timeline: moonlit undressing and an old bruise, pale firelight dancing across Marie's face from the burning bushes, the bed shoved as a barricade, plaster exploding around a wiry grabbing hand, the Lady in White squeezing through the wall gap. Blue-gray night palette.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Jack's Bedroom
description: Jack on the bed's edge with the flashlight, facing the wardrobe mirror; Ben sleeping on top of the covers beside him by morning. Alternate versions: on the opposite side of the mirrored landing, thin light under the door, game sounds within; the blanket fort and bedtime story; Ben bursting in to scoop the sleeping boy as the front door is smashed below. Warm child clutter shading into prophecy.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Corridor / Landing / Staircase
description: Ben's nightly route: easing the door shut, bat in hand, checking window locks and the triple-locked doors, "Click, click, click… The castle is secure. Middle-class decor buried under the perma-mess of a small child". In the siege: stairs taken two at a time over crunching glass, "shards glittering in the moonlight," something *humming* in the walls; The Mother suddenly upon him, ax handle to throat, lifting him off the ground. In the reversed timeline the geography mirrors itself — a door that should be the basement opens on a pantry. Practical tungsten and shadow.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Front Door
description: Triple locks tested nightly; in the siege, heavy fists pound and smash the wood, and The Mother slams into it, glass smashing, a hand reaching in for the lock as Ben's poker falls again and again (Sc. 118 cabin-parallel; house assault Sc. 215–216, 228). Cops barrel through it to find Ben gone. Close on locks, hands, splintering wood.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Kitchen
description: Marie's transparent equation easel standing amid child clutter; the toy car underfoot; crayon drawings on the fridge; the dregs of orange juice. In the alternate timeline: tomato sauce over pasta, mother and son waiting disciplined for grace — "You going to say grace?"; the screen door through which Marie sees her dead husband's face and *SCREAMS*. Warm domestic tones, morning window light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Living Room
description: Marie closing a door for privacy, someone else moving behind the glass paneling; the mantelpiece of "strange and formal" pictures, the Northrup proposal photo; the landline where Waif's voice arrives — "Old friend. Me and Ben go way back". Siren lights flooding across the IN MEMORIAM PHOTO of Ben. Comfortable middle-class warmth with something off in the pictures.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Basement
description: Light spilling in, broken by Ben's silhouette; a wall of tools hung from nails; the ax taken down; duct tape looping a flashlight to the handle; a box-cutter for the back pocket. The underworld of his own home. Cold utility light, deep shadow.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Driveway / Front Lawn
description: The drift up the driveway as the family emerges. In the siege: garden bushes on pale blue fire, The Mother in silent incantation, the Lady in White's grimy feet approaching; the dead Forester (dry CLICK, Sc. 230) and the screwdriver hot-wire under the hood while Marie holds the ax-line; the hand broken in the car door at escape. Overcast suburban light shattered by blue flame.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Suburban House — Reversed Interior
description: "Everything mirrored, reversed": the landing navigated wrong-footed, Jack's room on the opposite side, a pantry where the basement should be. A physical instantiation of timeline drift — play framing and layout, not distortion effects.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Intersection — Green-Light State
description: "They approach a BUSY INTERSECTION. Ben *just* makes the light before it changes" — Marie: "We'd have been trapped there for five minutes". Then the skyscraper-shadowed avenue, Jack's POV of a dark figure sweeping down a building's reflection, and "in a split second: the roof of the car caves in" — blood shockwave, airbag, horn, the rear-end impact. Gray daylight, mundane infrastructure, tragedy without landmark.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Intersection — Red-Light State
description: The final variation: "The lights turn RED… 'We'll be stuck here five minutes.' He puts the car in park" — Marie looks at the sky: "Looks like it'll be a nice day". Jack traces his finger on the glass; the camera drifts up from him, across the roof, "pulling back wider and wider until the busy crossroads fills the screen". Stillness as benediction; softer light than the green-state version.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — Approach Road
description: Cracked asphalt two-lane, "trees thick enough to choke the sunset"; the vista opening downhill, "cold lake glistens in low sun"; "patchwork of private docks, nestled among cattail marshes" and the BROKEN BOW sign. Low sun, long shadows, windshield POV.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — Main Street
description: "The main street of BROKEN BOW rolls past at thirty. Flags hang from shop fronts. Diner. Bank. Tackle shop. A barber sweeping his porch. Brick facades softened by lake winters". After the first reset: "more genteel and less run down". After the second: "Same street, different feel. Neon signs for cannabis outlets. A woman pushing a can trolley". Faded reds and brick browns, gray lake light — redressed per timeline.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — Last Chance Supply
description: "Coolers, pegboard tool racks, and a glass gun cabinet"; the ammo counter, the Catfish Catfood stand, a cooler of beers, Ricky at the TV, under-counter moonshine. The name is the joke and the thesis. Warm cluttered interior, tungsten retail light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — Second Last Chance Supply
description: The first reset's version: "Everything is slightly newer, less scrappy"; a backroom gunsmith bench; the receipt jotter reading SECOND LAST CHANCE SUPPLY and a sign that "appears used and worn, not new" — the Mandela effect in signage. Same layout, subtly upgraded dressing.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — T.R. General Supplies
description: The second reset's version: "the store layout is different again. No guns, but a large vape stand instead," T.R. behind the counter, cash only. Here Waif tries on shades and hat, tags still on — "It's not that easy… to choose" — and delivers "We're entangled. My world and yours, all wrapped up together. We share the same state". Fluorescent retail light; the same bones, a third skin.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — The Diner (facade)
description: Part of the Main Street drive-by only — diner, bank, tackle shop. *Canon note:* the fountain contains no diner interior; the "entangled" confrontation belongs to T.R. General Supplies. Use the diner as exterior texture: signage, window glow, gray lake light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — The Alley
description: "The alley between shops": a teenage boy at its mouth, Terry "deeper in the shadows… hands stuffed in jacket". The town's shadow economy in one corridor. Gray-brown grime, shallow light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — Outskirts
description: "Run-down homes - shacks, really - and patchwork trailers sprawling on the edge of deeper woods". Economic desperation behind the postcard. Overcast; muted, exhausted palette.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Broken Bow — Forest Turn / Forest Path
description: The turn Ben takes to test his tail: "The path is getting darker. No houses around. Maybe he's fucked up"; pickup and Chevrolet mirrored on the shoulder; the fight at the car — arm twisted, face to metal, the frisk, the buy; the Chevrolet's revving U-turn. Dark conifer corridor, two vehicles, no witnesses.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Scenic Stop / Scenic Pullout
description: Off-road pockets in the trees: where the pickup parks for the test-fire, and where Ben hides "shaded away from the road," head in hands, as a siren's blue-red sweeps the car and keeps going. Gravel, guardrail, screening pines.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Bridge / Dark Water
description: "We pull back from a whirlpool in the dark water"; Ben's truck crossing "a bridge over a fast-flowing river," his glance over the passing rail at the "watery abyss below". The camera returns after the Preston visit, drifting beyond the bridge "to look at the black water beneath". Suicide-inflected threshold imagery: iron truss or concrete span, black-green water, cold light.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Municipal Court Building
description: Not a courtroom drama — a paperwork one. The steps and glass doors Ben pushes through with a heavy gait; the conference room, two lawyers flanking Ben and Marie across a table; the cafeteria's canteen benches and two vending-machine coffees — "five bucks of coffee might save us a few hundred dollars in fees"; the corridor view THRU GLASS of papers being signed; the parking lot where Marie unscrews the cabin keys from her ring and Ben rolls up the window on "Be well". Cold institutional neutrals, fluorescent light, civic stone.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Preston Mansion / North Haven
description: The strangely empty gray freeway out; an old-money enclave of mansions "back from the road behind big gates and railings"; the chained-but-loose wrought-iron gate between stone pillars that Ben squeezes through, Colonial Revival mansion beyond, "no lights or signs of life"; the garden gravestone under leaves — Q.R. PRESTON, 1946–2014, NEXT TO HIS BELOVED, JOYCE. Interior: the kitchenette where Joyce leafs through the book. Autumn leaves, wrought iron, patrician decay.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Preston Mansion — The Lab
description: "Striplights flicker on… vintage radiology equipment. Capacitor banks"; deeper in, lathes, engineering equipment, "metal gun barrels under plastic" — Querry's armory-laboratory, stocked via "his old Los Alamos buddies" with isotopes his widow fears hazmat teams over. Part physics lab, part machine shop; humming fluorescents over dead equipment, dust sheets, cold steel.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Preston Mansion — Robbery (flashback)
description: The Sc. 239 flashes: masked Waif and Ben inside at night, the cosh blow, Joyce's hands bound, Querry emerging from the basement with the revolver, the trigger pulled on himself three times to nothing, the wrist snap, "Remember to load it next time, pops-" and the shot that works. 16mm flashback grain, candle-warm shadows, chaos in a grand interior.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: NYC — Skyscraper Corner Office
description: The Man before a floor-to-ceiling glass wall over New York City as Waif enters in a long coat and produces a revolver; the corrected memory: one shot shattering the glass into falling shards, Waif backing to the edge and letting herself fall; her tumble through the sky, coat ripped away by air friction "revealing jeans and shirt and sneakers underneath"; the impact — "her body smashing into the roof of the Forester". 16mm hand-cranked flashback register; steel blues and glass.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: NYC — Office Reception / Revolving Door
description: Waif in a long coat brushing past a secretary, pushing through a glass door; topdown from skyscraper to street, The Man emerging from a revolving door as the Door Guard grabs her and she SCREAMS after him. Corporate stone and glass, crowd flow, morning glare. 16mm grain.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: NYC — Busy Avenues
description: The crash avenue "shadowed by skyscrapers of glass and steel," reflections and gleaming buildings sliding past Jack's window; the flash-cut criminal past — Ben and Waif holding up a liquor store, "guns and chaos and masks," a convertible eyeballing the mansion. Handheld energy, steam and brake lights; degraded 16mm for the flashes.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: NYC — Waif & The Man's Home (flashback)
description: Dreamlike domestic fragments of the relationship: the bed where "he lifts her up with his arms, her whole weight resting upon him"; the driveway walk, her arm sliding under his; the hallway where she senses "the emptiness immediately. A missing presence"; the half-empty closet and rising worry. Soft-focus warmth curdling to absence; 16mm.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: NYC — The Motel
description: A cheap room with two single beds — one his, one hosting an open suitcase — early light through blinds; electric-razor shave in the bathroom; the TV table "surrounded by beer bottles" shoved into a trash bag; outside, an outdoor pool "covered in autumn leaves" and a neighbor on a deck chair as Ben hits the dumpster. Sodium light through blinds; transient beige.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Institution (Waif flashback)
description: "A long corridor. Murmurs of the unwell" where Waif huddles against a wall and The Man's backlit hand reaches down; corridor sweeping under a guard's stare; the patient room and the stolen keyring; the padded room and straitjacket where the faceless psychiatrist delivers the book; the alarm-screaming perimeter fence she scales. Fluorescent flicker, institutional green-gray, restraint hardware; 16mm degradation.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Hospital (Waif flashback)
description: The intensive-care bed, head swabbed in bandage, the nurse-shaped Mother staring from the doorway; pliers found under the pillow; the storage room where sheared copper sparks against her arm and throws her across the room. Hospital-clean turned uncanny; flickering fluorescents.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Freeway (Waif flashback)
description: Beyond the fence: Waif "lit by the lights of an 18 WHEELER. She primes herself, and- calmly steps in front of it"; the driver's cockpit brake-slam; her body ripped underneath; then the stretcher, the paramedic's "absolute miracle she's not dead," the ambulance. Headlight glare, wet asphalt, emergency strobe. 16mm.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: The Courthouse Steps
description: The Municipal Court Building exterior: Ben moving up the steps, "his gait heavy and slow," pushing through glass doors as his wife's lawyer's voice pre-laps the terms. Gray civic daylight, stone and handrails.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Pale Blue Fire (phenomenon)
description: "A pale blue shade, a strange pattern like St. Elmo's Fire" — burning steady through wind and downpour, no heat to a reaching hand, extinguishing without smoke or char. It consumes the suburban garden bushes as The Mother incants, glows through shutter-gaps as the Others mass, lights the climactic yard massacre, and dies when the transformed Waif clicks her fingers. Render as cold plasma, never campfire orange: blue-white cores, faint violet edges, the only saturated cold accent in the film's palette.
notes: New from fountain analysis — Claude Opus

name: Ben (base)
description: Jonathan Rhys Meyers — cast. Broad-shouldered man, mid-40s, mechanic's build with calloused capable hands. Dark receding hair, face almost always unshaven, heavy around the eyes with deep-set tired eyes and strong jaw working with suppressed emotion. Multiple tattoos across torso and arms — most notably a faded black-ink Ace of Hearts on his inner wrist, the mark of a New Mexico crew. Wears long sleeves specifically to hide his tattoos. Earth-tone palette: faded blues, grays, browns. Contemporary working-class American.
notes: Base description — invariant physical traits. Tag alongside scene-specific variant for complete prompt injection.
